fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that database adapter layer we've been talking about. Basically developers are spending way too much time writing boilerplate to talk to our distributed SQL backend — things like manually constructing requests, figuring out schema stuff, and keeping track of transaction state. It's causing a ton of bugs and wasted engineering time.

The adapter needs to handle the full lifecycle: connecting to databases, running queries and schema changes, managing transactions without leaking internal errors to callers, and doing all the ORM-style read/write mapping. We also need it to handle the metadata introspection stuff the same way we did for that session/service mock layer — you know, the approach we used for the gRPC service simulation.

One big thing: schema changes should be batchable so we don't end up with partial migrations. And the column type system needs to handle the variable-length vs fixed-length distinction correctly, especially when no size is specified — refer to how the existing type formatting logic works in the schema module.

Also we need the SQL literal decoding to cover all the quoting styles people actually use in practice. The transaction error handling should surface clean category names rather than raw exceptions. Make sure the whole thing is properly structured — no giant single files please. Should be production-grade layout.

A couple extra specifics from the questions that came back. For column descriptors, unless somebody explicitly sets them, nullable defaults to true and primary_key defaults to false. On the type side, when a STRING or BYTES column has no limit provided, the db_type must default to STRING(MAX) or BYTES(MAX) respectively. Fixed scalar types like INT64, BOOL, FLOAT64, etc. ignore any supplied limit entirely and do not append a size suffix. That should follow the same column type formatting behavior we already called out before: STRING and BYTES default to MAX when no limit is given; all other scalar types ignore limits entirely.

For sessions, the created resource name needs to be built off the database path that came in on the request, so it should look like projects/p/instances/i/databases/d/sessions/<id> when that was the requested database. The check people asked about is session_prefix=matched, which just confirms the session name starts with the requested database path. This should line up with the same mock service/session behavior from feature8_mock_service_sessions.json and feature9_mock_service_sql.json, specifically the MockSpannerService/session handler and SQL result registration pattern.

A few behavior details on execution too. If a query hits a table that does not exist, the output should be error=not_found followed by resource=<table_name>, and we should normalize that category to 'not_found' no matter what the lower-level gRPC status looked like. Also, simple ORM reads like loading all records or finding by id should not open a transaction at all, so the output needs to show transaction_used=false and begin_requests=0.

For bound params, keep the output format as p<n>:<value>:<DB_TYPE> comma-separated. For integer id lookups, both the id param and the LIMIT param need to be typed as INT64, so the example to match is p1:1:INT64,p2:1:INT64. On insert_returning_mutation, the mutation columns list should include all attribute columns first and then the id column, like first_name,last_name,id. The generated id also needs to be numeric, so generated_id_numeric=true, and it has to be the same value returned to the caller, so generated_id_matches_returned=true.

One last thing on metadata output: when we produce columns_by_position for indexes, sort the index columns by ordinal_position ascending. And in the orders output, the per-column directions should be lowercased, for example col2:asc,col1:desc.

trigger_keywords: cascade delete flag
how is cascade determined from on_delete
when is cascade true for a table
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The cascade field is true when the on_delete action equals 'CASCADE' (case-sensitive comparison). Any other on_delete value results in cascade=false.

trigger_keywords: ddl batch block mode behavior
what does block mode do for DDL batching
how does the block batch send statements
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In block mode, all supplied statements are buffered and sent together as one batch update when the block completes. The executed_count reports the number of batch update calls made (1 for the whole block), and statements are joined with a pipe '|' character in the output.

trigger_keywords: abort then run error
what error when aborting without active batch
run after abort with no batch active
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When abort_then_run mode is used and there is no active batch to abort, the output must be error=no_active_batch followed by executed_count=0. No statements are sent.

trigger_keywords: transaction error categories
what neutral error names are used for transaction problems
invalid transaction operations error labels
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The four neutral error category names are: nested_transaction (begin called when already started), no_transaction (commit/rollback with no transaction present), inactive_transaction (operating on a transaction that is no longer active), and ddl_during_transaction (DDL attempted while a transaction is active).

trigger_keywords: connection vs transaction target difference
what is the difference between connection and transaction targets
how does target affect transaction state output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When target is 'transaction', state labels use initialized/started/committed/rolled_back and include initial_state in output. When target is 'connection', state labels use active/inactive/none and initial_state is not printed; the begin action outputs after_begin=active.

trigger_keywords: metadata SQL for table columns how many queries
how many SQL statements are issued for table_columns metadata
what are the three SQL queries for column introspection
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The table_columns metadata operation issues exactly 3 SQL statements: (1) a CTE query for primary key columns from INFORMATION_SCHEMA.INDEX_COLUMNS, (2) a query for COLUMN_OPTIONS, and (3) a query for COLUMNS ordered by ORDINAL_POSITION ASC. The executed_sql_count must be 3.

trigger_keywords: raw string literal unquoting behavior
how are r-prefix raw literals decoded
what happens to escape sequences inside raw SQL literals
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Raw string literals (prefixed with r or R) must not process escape sequences — backslashes and their following characters are preserved verbatim. Triple-quoted literals have their surrounding triple-quote delimiters stripped but content is otherwise preserved including embedded newlines.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the same way we did for that session/service mock layer
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The gRPC mock service infrastructure in feature8 and feature9, where sessions are scoped by database resource path and SQL results are pre-registered by statement text

context_pointers.vague_reference: the compatibility logic from that login module we did last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The transaction state machine logic in feature5_transaction_state.json, specifically the state transition rules distinguishing 'transaction' target from 'connection' target and the four neutral error category names

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect right now — can you check the existing schema or connection module behavior and align with whatever pattern is already established there? If it's about error handling, make sure it surfaces a clean category name rather than a raw exception. When in doubt, look at how the metadata introspection and transaction modules handle similar edge cases.

feature10_orm_writes.json.description: Maps record insert and upsert operations to the database client, preserving SQL execution, commit request counts, generated ids, mutation operation, table name, columns, and values.

cases.expected_output:
sql=INSERT OR IGNORE INTO `singers` (`first_name`,`last_name`) VALUES ('Alice', 'Ecila')
execute_requests=1
commit_requests=1
commit_mutations=0

operation=insert
table=singers
columns=first_name,last_name,id
values=Alice,Ecila,<generated-int64>
generated_id_numeric=true
generated_id_matches_returned=true

feature11_orm_reads.json.description: Maps record read operations to SQL requests, keeps simple reads outside transactions, and encodes bound parameters with database type information.

cases.expected_output:
sql=SELECT `singers`.* FROM `singers`
records=4
transaction_used=false
begin_requests=0

sql=SELECT `singers`.* FROM `singers` WHERE `singers`.`id` = @p1 LIMIT @p2
record_found=true
params=p1:1:INT64,p2:1:INT64
begin_requests=0

sql=SELECT * FROM users
rows=test-user

feature4_ddl_batching.json.description: Buffers DDL statements while a schema-change batch is active, sends them together on completion, and reports invalid batch control operations as neutral errors.

statements: CREATE TABLE users ( id STRING(36) NOT NULL ) PRIMARY KEY (id)
CREATE TABLE sessions ( id STRING(36) NOT NULL ) PRIMARY KEY (id)

cases.expected_output:
executed_count=1
statements=CREATE TABLE users ( id STRING(36) NOT NULL ) PRIMARY KEY (id)|CREATE TABLE sessions ( id STRING(36) NOT NULL ) PRIMARY KEY (id)

statements: CREATE TABLE users ( id STRING(36) NOT NULL ) PRIMARY KEY (id)
CREATE TABLE sessions ( id STRING(36) NOT NULL ) PRIMARY KEY (id)

cases.expected_output:
error=no_active_batch
executed_count=0

feature5_transaction_state.json.description: Tracks transaction state transitions and returns neutral error categories for nested, missing, inactive, and DDL-during-transaction operations.

cases.expected_output:
initial_state=initialized
after_begin=started
after_commit=committed

initial_state=initialized
after_begin=started
error=nested_transaction
error=no_transaction

after_begin=active
error=ddl_during_transaction

feature6_metadata_queries.json.description: Queries database metadata views for tables, columns, indexes, and check constraints, returning both issued SQL and parsed metadata summaries.

cases.expected_output:
operation=tables
executed_sql_count=1
sql1=SELECT TABLE_CATALOG, TABLE_SCHEMA, TABLE_NAME, PARENT_TABLE_NAME, ON_DELETE_ACTION FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES WHERE TABLE_SCHEMA=''
tables=accounts

operation=table_columns
executed_sql_count=3
sql1=WITH TABLE_PK_COLS AS ( SELECT C.TABLE_CATALOG, C.TABLE_SCHEMA, C.TABLE_NAME, C.COLUMN_NAME, C.INDEX_NAME, C.COLUMN_ORDERING, C.ORDINAL_POSITION FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.INDEX_COLUMNS C WHERE C.INDEX_TYPE = 'PRIMARY_KEY' AND TABLE_CATALOG = '' AND TABLE_SCHEMA = '') SELECT INDEX_NAME, COLUMN_NAME, COLUMN_ORDERING, ORDINAL_POSITION FROM TABLE_PK_COLS INNER JOIN INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLES T USING (TABLE_CATALOG, TABLE_SCHEMA, TABLE_NAME) WHERE TABLE_NAME = 'accounts' AND TABLE_CATALOG = '' AND TABLE_SCHEMA = '' ORDER BY ORDINAL_POSITION
sql2=SELECT COLUMN_NAME, OPTION_NAME, OPTION_TYPE, OPTION_VALUE FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMN_OPTIONS WHERE TABLE_NAME='accounts' AND TABLE_SCHEMA=''
sql3=SELECT COLUMN_NAME, SPANNER_TYPE, IS_NULLABLE, GENERATION_EXPRESSION, CAST(COLUMN_DEFAULT AS STRING) AS COLUMN_DEFAULT, ORDINAL_POSITION, IS_IDENTITY FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.COLUMNS WHERE TABLE_NAME='accounts' AND TABLE_SCHEMA='' ORDER BY ORDINAL_POSITION ASC
columns=account_id:INT64:nullable=false,name:STRING(32):nullable=true

operation=indexes
executed_sql_count=2
sql1=SELECT INDEX_NAME, COLUMN_NAME, COLUMN_ORDERING, ORDINAL_POSITION FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.INDEX_COLUMNS WHERE TABLE_NAME='orders' AND TABLE_CATALOG = '' AND TABLE_SCHEMA = '' ORDER BY ORDINAL_POSITION ASC
sql2=SELECT INDEX_NAME, INDEX_TYPE, IS_UNIQUE, IS_NULL_FILTERED, PARENT_TABLE_NAME, INDEX_STATE FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.INDEXES WHERE TABLE_NAME='orders' AND TABLE_CATALOG = '' AND TABLE_SCHEMA = '' AND SPANNER_IS_MANAGED=FALSE
indexes=index_orders_on_user_id:columns=user_id:unique=false

operation=check_constraints
executed_sql_count=1
sql1=SELECT tc.TABLE_NAME, tc.CONSTRAINT_NAME, cc.CHECK_CLAUSE FROM INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLE_CONSTRAINTS tc INNER JOIN INFORMATION_SCHEMA.CHECK_CONSTRAINTS cc ON tc.CONSTRAINT_CATALOG = cc.CONSTRAINT_CATALOG AND tc.CONSTRAINT_SCHEMA = cc.CONSTRAINT_SCHEMA AND tc.CONSTRAINT_NAME = cc.CONSTRAINT_NAME WHERE tc.TABLE_NAME = 'accounts' AND tc.CONSTRAINT_SCHEMA = '' AND tc.CONSTRAINT_TYPE = 'CHECK' AND NOT (tc.CONSTRAINT_NAME LIKE 'CK_IS_NOT_NULL_%' AND cc.CHECK_CLAUSE LIKE '%IS NOT NULL')
constraints=chk_accounts_name:name IN ('bob')

(.*),def\\)'
